text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old male with newly diagnosed nodular melanoma of the upper back, Breslow thickness 6.1 mm, ulceration present, Clark level V, with known right axillary lymphadenopathy on clinical examination. Staging evaluation for metastatic disease. BRAF V600E-positive by immunohistochemistry.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of iohexol contrast material (90 mL IV, injected at 3 mL/sec via right antecubital vein). Images were acquired in the portal venous phase through the abdomen and pelvis. Standard dose chest CT was performed without breath-hold artifact. Reconstructions performed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. No prior CT studies available for direct comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation identified. No ground-glass opacity. The trachea is midline. Central airways are patent to the segmental level. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleura: No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The aorta and great vessels are unremarkable in caliber. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy by size criteria. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses. The thymus is atrophic and unremarkable for age.

Hila: No hilar lymphadenopathy. Hilar vascular structures are symmetric.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: Bilateral axillary regions demonstrate significant lymphadenopathy (detailed below under lymph nodes). No osseous lesion or cortical destruction identified on soft tissue windows within the ribs, sternum, or thoracic spine. The imaged breast parenchyma is unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:

Right Axilla: There is a large conglomerate nodal mass in the right axilla measuring approximately 4.8 x 3.9 cm in the axial plane (series 3, image 48). The mass demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement with a region of central low attenuation consistent with necrosis. Several discrete enlarged satellite nodes are seen within the right axillary fat, the largest measuring 1.6 cm in short axis. The conglomerate mass abuts but does not appear to invade the adjacent right pectoralis minor muscle or chest wall. No vascular encasement identified.

Left Axilla: Mildly enlarged left axillary nodes, the largest measuring 1.1 cm in short axis with preserved fatty hilum, indeterminate but noted in the context of known metastatic disease.

Cervical/Supraclavicular (imaged on superior chest cuts): Bilateral low cervical and supraclavicular lymph nodes are visible at the thoracic inlet. On the left, there are two discrete enlarged nodes at the level of the left jugular chain (level II-III region, imaged on lower neck cuts) measuring up to 2.2 cm in short axis, with rounded morphology and loss of normal fatty hilum, suspicious for nodal metastases. Right low cervical nodes are not enlarged by size criteria.

No mediastinal, subcarinal, or internal mammary nodal enlargement is identified.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. There are at least six discrete hepatic lesions distributed throughout both lobes demonstrating low attenuation on the portal venous phase relative to background hepatic parenchyma. The dominant lesion is situated in segment VII and measures 3.1 x 2.7 cm (series 5, image 72); it demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with central hypodensity, a pattern that raises concern for a hypervascular metastasis with central necrosis. A second notable lesion in segment II measures 1.8 x 1.5 cm with similar enhancement characteristics. Additional smaller hypodense lesions are identified in segments V (measuring approximately 0.9 cm), segment VIII (0.7 cm), and two lesions in the right lobe measuring 0.6 cm and 0.5 cm respectively. This multi-focal pattern and enhancement profile is consistent with melanoma metastases in the appropriate clinical setting. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow implied by enhancement. The hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No gallstones. No wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid.

Spleen: Normal in size at 10.8 cm craniocaudal. Homogeneous parenchymal enhancement without focal lesion. No splenic metastases identified.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in size and enhancement throughout its entirety. No ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands: Both kidneys are normal in size and enhance symmetrically. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology with no nodularity or enlargement. No adrenal lesion suspicious for metastasis.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach and visualized bowel are unremarkable without focal wall thickening, mass effect, or obstruction. The appendix is visualized and appears normal.

Retroperitoneum and Vasculature: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. No retroperitoneal or para-aortic lymphadenopathy by size criteria. The inferior vena cava is patent.

Ascites: No free intraperitoneal fluid identified.

PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Common iliac, external iliac, and inguinal nodal chains are within normal limits bilaterally.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is adequately distended with smooth wall contour. No intraluminal lesion.

Male Reproductive: The prostate gland is normal in volume for age without discrete lesion. No seminal vesicle abnormality.

Bony Pelvis: No lytic or sclerotic bony lesion identified on soft tissue windows. The sacrum and iliac wings appear intact.

SOFT TISSUES OF THE TRUNK:

Multiple subcutaneous nodules are identified along the anterior and posterior trunk as well as the lateral chest wall bilaterally. These range in size from approximately 0.8 cm to 1.4 cm in greatest dimension. They are well-circumscribed, slightly hyperdense compared to surrounding subcutaneous fat, without calcification. At least five to six discrete nodules are identified at the level of the imaged thorax and upper abdomen, with additional lesions partially visualized at the margins of coverage. In the clinical context of known ulcerated melanoma with lymphovascular invasion at the primary site, these are highly suspicious for cutaneous/subcutaneous satellite or in-transit metastases.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:

No definitive bone metastasis is identified on the available soft tissue windows. The vertebral bodies, ribs, pelvis, and proximal femora appear intact without cortical disruption or marrow replacement signal. Dedicated bone windows and/or nuclear medicine bone scan or PET would be of value for complete osseous staging.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Extensive metastatic melanoma consistent with Stage IV (M1c) disease, with involvement of multiple organ systems as described below.

2. Large right axillary conglomerate nodal mass measuring approximately 4.8 x 3.9 cm with central necrosis, along with several additional right axillary satellite nodes. Consistent with bulky regional nodal metastatic disease.

3. Left axillary and left cervical (level II-III) lymphadenopathy, the latter measuring up to 2.2 cm with loss of normal nodal architecture, suspicious for metastatic involvement.

4. At least six discrete hepatic metastases throughout both lobes, largest measuring 3.1 x 2.7 cm in segment VII, with enhancement pattern and morphology consistent with melanoma metastases. No biliary obstruction or hepatic vascular compromise.

5. Multiple subcutaneous and soft tissue nodules along the trunk (at least 5-6 discrete lesions, 0.8-1.4 cm), highly suspicious for cutaneous/in-transit metastases in this clinical context.

6. No pulmonary nodules or parenchymal lesions.

7. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.

8. No adrenal lesions. No bony destructive lesions on soft tissue windowing.

9. No ascites or pleural effusion.

Correlation with PET-CT for complete metabolic disease characterization and brain MRI for intracranial staging is advised as part of complete workup for newly diagnosed metastatic melanoma.

Dictated and electronically signed by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging Section
